## Finished in  0.6 seconds.
## Initial log joint probability = -46.8513 
## Optimization terminated normally:  
##   Convergence detected: relative gradient magnitude is below tolerance 
## Finished in  0.6 seconds.

## Finished in  0.7 seconds.

## Finished in  0.6 seconds.

## Finished in  0.6 seconds.
## Initial log joint probability = -50.3878 
## Optimization terminated normally:

## Finished in  0.6 seconds.

## Optimization terminated normally:

## Initial log joint probability = -44.6438 
## Optimization terminated normally:  
##   Convergence detected: relative gradient magnitude is below tolerance 
## Finished in  0.6 seconds.
# 2. Extract MULTILEVEL (partially pooled) posterior means and join
recovery_ml <- agent_params_ml |>
  mutate(
    est_rho   = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_kappa = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("kappa[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_wd    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("weight_direct[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_ws    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_ml[[paste0("weight_social[", .x, "]")]]))
  ) |>
  left_join(unpooled_ests, by = "agent_id")

# 3. Calculate population means (the "center of gravity")
pop_rho_true   <- plogis(pop_logit_rho_mean)
pop_kappa_true <- exp(pop_log_kappa_mean)
pop_wd_true    <- pop_rho_true * pop_kappa_true
pop_ws_true    <- (1 - pop_rho_true) * pop_kappa_true

# 4. Determine a common maximum scale for wd and ws plots
max_w <- max(c(recovery_ml$weight_direct, recovery_ml$est_wd, recovery_ml$unpooled_wd,
               recovery_ml$weight_social, recovery_ml$est_ws, recovery_ml$unpooled_ws)) * 1.05

# 5. Build plots: Arrow from Unpooled (hollow) to Pooled (solid)
p_rho <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = rho)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_rho_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = rho, y = unpooled_rho, yend = est_rho), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_rho), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_rho), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#4575b4") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: ρ (relative allocation)", x = "True ρ", y = "Estimated ρ") 

p_kappa <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = kappa)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_kappa_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = kappa, y = unpooled_kappa, yend = est_kappa), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_kappa), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_kappa), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#d73027") +
  labs(title = "Recovery: κ (total evidence scaling)", x = "True κ", y = "Estimated κ") 

p_wd <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = weight_direct)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_wd_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = weight_direct, y = unpooled_wd, yend = est_wd), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_wd), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_wd), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#4daf4a") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max_w), ylim = c(0, max_w)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: w_d (derived)", x = "True w_d", y = "Estimated w_d")

p_ws <- ggplot(recovery_ml, aes(x = weight_social)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pop_ws_true, linetype = "dotted", colour = "black", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = weight_social, y = unpooled_ws, yend = est_ws), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(y = unpooled_ws), shape = 1, size = 2.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(y = est_ws), size = 3, alpha = 0.9, colour = "#984ea3") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max_w), ylim = c(0, max_w)) +
  labs(title = "Recovery: w_s (derived)", x = "True w_s", y = "Estimated w_s")

(p_rho | p_kappa) / (p_wd | p_ws) +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Multilevel WBA Parameter Recovery & Shrinkage",
    subtitle = "Arrows trace shrinkage from Unpooled MAP estimates (hollow) to Multilevel Posterior Means (solid).\nNotice how pooling pulls extreme estimates toward the population mean (dotted horizontal lines)."
  )

Look for three features. First, \(\rho\) recovery should be tight — this parameter is well-identified from conflict trials, just as in the single-agent case. Second, \(\kappa\) recovery will be noisier — the total evidence scale is harder to pin down from binary outcomes. Third, agents at the extremes should be pulled toward the centre relative to their true values. This is partial pooling in action: the hierarchical prior regularises extreme estimates. The strength of this shrinkage depends on how informative each agent’s data are relative to the population distribution.

10.22 Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC): Multilevel Models

SBC is more critical for multilevel models than for single-agent models. The hierarchical posterior geometry — with its potential funnels, correlations, and partial-pooling dynamics — creates subtle failure modes that standard MCMC diagnostics may miss. We run SBC for both the ML-WBA and ML-PBA.

n_sbc_ml <- 200  # Reduced from single-agent count due to computational cost

if (regenerate_simulations) {

  # ── ML-WBA SBC Generator ──────────────────────────────────────────
  sbc_gen_ml_wba <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(n_agents_sbc, ev, n_reps) {
      J <- n_agents_sbc
      
      # Draw population parameters from priors
      mu_1     <- rnorm(1, 0, 1.5)       # logit_rho mean
      mu_2     <- rnorm(1, log(2), 0.5)   # log_kappa mean
      sigma_1  <- rexp(1, 2)
      sigma_2  <- rexp(1, 2)
      
      # LKJ(2) correlation: draw from marginal Beta, rescale
      r_raw    <- rbeta(1, 2, 2)
      Omega_12 <- 2 * r_raw - 1
      
      # Construct Cholesky of correlation matrix
      L11 <- 1; L21 <- Omega_12; L22 <- sqrt(1 - Omega_12^2)
      
      # Draw individual parameters (NCP)
      z <- matrix(rnorm(2 * J), nrow = 2)
      theta_raw <- diag(c(sigma_1, sigma_2)) %*% rbind(
        L11 * z[1,],
        L21 * z[1,] + L22 * z[2,]
      )
      
      rho_j   <- plogis(mu_1 + theta_raw[1,])
      kappa_j <- exp(mu_2 + theta_raw[2,])
      wd_j    <- rho_j * kappa_j
      ws_j    <- (1 - rho_j) * kappa_j
      
      # Generate trial data for each agent
      all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
        agent_ev <- ev |> slice(rep(seq_len(n()), times = n_reps))
        N_j <- nrow(agent_ev)
        
        alpha_p <- 0.5 + wd_j[j] * agent_ev$blue1 + ws_j[j] * agent_ev$blue2
        beta_p  <- 0.5 + wd_j[j] * (agent_ev$total1 - agent_ev$blue1) +
                         ws_j[j] * (agent_ev$total2 - agent_ev$blue2)
        p_blue  <- rbeta(N_j, alpha_p, beta_p)
        
        agent_ev |> mutate(
          agent_id = j,
          choice   = rbinom(N_j, 1, p_blue)
        )
      })
      
      list(
        variables = list(
          `mu[1]`      = mu_1,
          `mu[2]`      = mu_2,
          `sigma[1]`   = sigma_1,
          `sigma[2]`   = sigma_2,
          `Omega[1,2]` = Omega_12
        ),
        generated = list(
          N        = nrow(all_data),
          J        = J,
          agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
          choice   = all_data$choice,
          blue1    = all_data$blue1,
          total1   = all_data$total1,
          blue2    = all_data$blue2,
          total2   = all_data$total2
        )
      )
    },
    n_agents_sbc = 10,  # Fewer agents for SBC speed
    ev           = evidence_combinations,
    n_reps       = 2
  )
  
  sbc_datasets_ml_wba <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_ml_wba, n_sbc_ml)
  
  sbc_backend_ml_wba <- SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_ml_wba,
    chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,
    parallel_chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  
  sbc_results_ml_wba <- compute_SBC(sbc_datasets_ml_wba, sbc_backend_ml_wba,
                                     keep_fits = FALSE)
  saveRDS(sbc_results_ml_wba, "simmodels/ch9_sbc_ml_wba.rds")

  # ── ML-PBA SBC Generator ──────────────────────────────────────────
  sbc_gen_ml_pba <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(n_agents_sbc, ev, n_reps) {
      J <- n_agents_sbc
      
      mu_logit_p    <- rnorm(1, 0, 1.5)
      sigma_logit_p <- rexp(1, 2)
      z_p           <- rnorm(J)
      p_j           <- plogis(mu_logit_p + sigma_logit_p * z_p)
      
      all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
        agent_ev <- ev |> slice(rep(seq_len(n()), times = n_reps))
        N_j <- nrow(agent_ev)
        
        alpha_p <- 0.5 + p_j[j] * agent_ev$blue1 + (1 - p_j[j]) * agent_ev$blue2
        beta_p  <- 0.5 + p_j[j] * (agent_ev$total1 - agent_ev$blue1) +
                         (1 - p_j[j]) * (agent_ev$total2 - agent_ev$blue2)
        p_blue  <- rbeta(N_j, alpha_p, beta_p)
        
        agent_ev |> mutate(
          agent_id = j,
          choice   = rbinom(N_j, 1, p_blue)
        )
      })
      
      list(
        variables = list(
          mu_logit_p    = mu_logit_p,
          sigma_logit_p = sigma_logit_p
        ),
        generated = list(
          N        = nrow(all_data),
          J        = J,
          agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
          choice   = all_data$choice,
          blue1    = all_data$blue1,
          total1   = all_data$total1,
          blue2    = all_data$blue2,
          total2   = all_data$total2
        )
      )
    },
    n_agents_sbc = 10,
    ev           = evidence_combinations,
    n_reps       = 2
  )
  
  sbc_datasets_ml_pba <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_ml_pba, n_sbc_ml)
  
  sbc_backend_ml_pba <- SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_ml_pba,
    chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,
    parallel_chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  
  sbc_results_ml_pba <- compute_SBC(sbc_datasets_ml_pba, sbc_backend_ml_pba,
                                     keep_fits = FALSE)
  saveRDS(sbc_results_ml_pba, "simmodels/ch9_sbc_ml_pba.rds")
  
} else {
  sbc_results_ml_wba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_sbc_ml_wba.rds")
  sbc_results_ml_pba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_sbc_ml_pba.rds")
}

# Plot SBC calibration
p_sbc_ml_wba <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(
  sbc_results_ml_wba,
  variables = c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]")
) + labs(title = "SBC Calibration: ML-WBA (population parameters)",
         subtitle = paste0("N = ", n_sbc_ml, " simulations. Lines inside bands = calibrated."))

p_sbc_ml_pba <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(
  sbc_results_ml_pba,
  variables = c("mu_logit_p", "sigma_logit_p")
) + labs(title = "SBC Calibration: ML-PBA (population parameters)",
         subtitle = paste0("N = ", n_sbc_ml, " simulations."))

p_sbc_ml_wba / p_sbc_ml_pba

If any parameter’s ECDF difference line escapes the grey bands, the posterior for that parameter is systematically miscalibrated. Common failure modes for multilevel models: (1) \(\sigma\) dipping below the band near 0, indicating the hierarchical prior’s boundary repulsion is too weak; (2) the correlation \(\Omega_{1,2}\) showing asymmetric miscalibration, indicating the LKJ prior interacts poorly with the NCP geometry. If SBC passes, we have a global certificate that the Stan sampler is unbiased across the entire joint prior space.

10.22.1 Global Parameter Recovery from SBC

ml_wba_sbc_recovery <- sbc_results_ml_wba$stats |>
  filter(variable %in% c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]")) |>
  mutate(model = "ML-WBA")

ml_pba_sbc_recovery <- sbc_results_ml_pba$stats |>
  filter(variable %in% c("mu_logit_p", "sigma_logit_p")) |>
  mutate(model = "ML-PBA")

sbc_ml_recovery_df <- bind_rows(ml_wba_sbc_recovery, ml_pba_sbc_recovery)

ggplot(sbc_ml_recovery_df, aes(x = simulated_value, y = median)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95), alpha = 0.2, width = 0) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5, color = "#4575b4") +
  facet_wrap(model ~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Global Parameter Recovery: Multilevel SBC",
    subtitle = "Each point = one simulated population. Error bars = 90% CI.",
    x = "True (simulated) value",
    y = "Posterior median"
  ) +
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray95", color = NA))

10.23 Multilevel Model Comparison: Three-Way LOO

Following the procedure from the single-agent comparison, we compare all three multilevel models via PSIS-LOO. Because the simulated population has agents with varying weights, we expect the ML-WBA to outperform the ML-SBA. Whether the ML-PBA or ML-WBA wins depends on whether the population s \(\kappa\) variation provides sufficient predictive benefit to offset the additional complexity.

loo_ml_sba <- loo(fit_ml_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_ml_pba <- loo(fit_ml_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_ml_wba <- loo(fit_ml_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))

cat("=== Multilevel Model Comparison: ML-SBA vs. ML-PBA vs. ML-WBA ===\n")
## === Multilevel Model Comparison: ML-SBA vs. ML-PBA vs. ML-WBA ===
ml_comparison <- loo_compare(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_ml_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_ml_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_ml_wba)
)
print(ml_comparison)
##        elpd_diff se_diff
## ML-SBA  0.0       0.0   
## ML-WBA -1.9       1.0   
## ML-PBA -2.7       2.0
ml_weights <- loo_model_weights(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_ml_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_ml_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_ml_wba),
  method = "stacking"
)
cat("\nBayesian stacking weights:\n")
## 
## Bayesian stacking weights:
print(ml_weights)
## Method: stacking
## ------
##        weight
## ML-SBA 0.987 
## ML-PBA 0.013 
## ML-WBA 0.000

Because the simulated agents were drawn from a population with varying \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\) (not all equal to 1), the ML-WBA should outperform the ML-SBA. The ELPD difference quantifies the predictive cost of assuming all agents are perfect Bayesians when they are not. However, given we set the simulation to be close to the SBA (balanced weighting), the ML-SBA ends up winning.

Note that this is trial-level LOO: it measures generalisation to new trials from known agents. For the clinically relevant question — can we predict a new participant’s behaviour from the population distribution alone? — we would need agent-level K-fold cross-validation, as discussed in Chapter 7.

10.23.1 Visualising Pointwise ELPD Differences

To understand where the ML-WBA gains its advantage over the ML-SBA, we plot pointwise ELPD differences as a function of evidence conflict — the same diagnostic technique from the single-agent section.

pw_ml <- tibble(
  obs       = seq_len(stan_data_ml$N),
  agent_id  = stan_data_ml$agent_id,
  blue1     = stan_data_ml$blue1,
  blue2     = stan_data_ml$blue2,
  elpd_wba  = loo_ml_wba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_sba  = loo_ml_sba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_pba  = loo_ml_pba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_diff_wba_sba = elpd_wba - elpd_sba,
  elpd_diff_wba_pba = elpd_wba - elpd_pba
) |>
  left_join(
    agent_params_ml |> select(agent_id, rho, kappa),
    by = "agent_id"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    prop_direct       = blue1 / 8,
    prop_social       = blue2 / 3,
    evidence_conflict = abs(prop_direct - prop_social),
    rho_group = cut(rho, breaks = c(0, 0.35, 0.65, 1),
                    labels = c("Social-leaning", "Balanced", "Direct-leaning"))
  )

# WBA vs SBA, coloured by agent type
ggplot(pw_ml, aes(x = evidence_conflict, y = elpd_diff_wba_sba)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = rho_group), size = 1.5, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
              colour = "midnightblue", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ rho_group) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Social-leaning" = "#377eb8",
               "Balanced"       = "#4daf4a",
               "Direct-leaning" = "#e41a1c")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Pointwise ELPD: ML-WBA vs. ML-SBA",
    subtitle = "Faceted by agent type. Advantage grows with evidence conflict for non-balanced agents.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference (ML-WBA − ML-SBA)",
    colour = "Agent type"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

10.24 Model Recovery: Multilevel Level

Model recovery at the multilevel level asks: if data are generated by a specific multilevel model, can our comparison pipeline correctly identify it? We test three scenarios, paralleling the single-agent analysis.

10.24.1 Specific Scenarios

Before the full simulation, we examine three instructive scenarios:

  1. SBA-Mimic Population: All agents have \(\rho = 0.5, \kappa = 2.0\) (i.e., \(w_d = w_s = 1\)) with zero individual variation. The population is the SBA.

  2. PBA Population: Agents vary in \(\rho\) (drawn from a population) but all have \(\kappa = 1.0\). The total evidence budget is fixed; only allocation varies.

  3. Full WBA Population: Agents vary in both \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\). The full hierarchical model is needed.

# Helper: generate multilevel data from a parameter specification
generate_ml_data <- function(agent_rhos, agent_kappas, ev, n_reps = 2) {
  J <- length(agent_rhos)
  all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
    wd <- agent_rhos[j] * agent_kappas[j]
    ws <- (1 - agent_rhos[j]) * agent_kappas[j]
    generate_agent_decisions(wd, ws, ev, n_samples = n_reps) |>
      mutate(agent_id = j, total1 = 8L, total2 = 3L)
  })
  list(
    N = nrow(all_data), J = J,
    agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
    choice   = all_data$choice,
    blue1    = all_data$blue1,
    total1   = all_data$total1,
    blue2    = all_data$blue2,
    total2   = all_data$total2
  )
}

J_scen <- 15

# Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic (no individual variation)
scen1_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = rep(0.5, J_scen),
  agent_kappas = rep(2.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 2: PBA Population (rho varies, kappa = 1)
scen2_rhos <- plogis(rnorm(J_scen, 0.5, 0.5))
scen2_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = scen2_rhos,
  agent_kappas = rep(1.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 3: Full WBA population (both vary)
scen3_draws <- MASS::mvrnorm(J_scen, mu = c(0.3, log(2.5)),
                              Sigma = matrix(c(0.5, -0.15, -0.15, 0.2), 2))
scen3_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = plogis(scen3_draws[, 1]),
  agent_kappas = exp(scen3_draws[, 2]),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)
fit_ml_scenario <- function(stan_data, label) {
  cat(sprintf("\n--- Fitting scenario: %s ---\n", label))

  sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.9,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )

  # Return pre-computed LOO objects, not the raw fits
  list(
    SBA = loo(sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    PBA = loo(pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    WBA = loo(wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
  )
}

if (regenerate_simulations) {
  loos_scen1 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen1_data, "SBA-Mimic Population")
  loos_scen2 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen2_data, "PBA Population")
  loos_scen3 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen3_data, "Full WBA Population")
  saveRDS(list(s1 = loos_scen1, s2 = loos_scen2, s3 = loos_scen3),
          "simmodels/ch9_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
} else {
  loos_all   <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
  loos_scen1 <- loos_all$s1
  loos_scen2 <- loos_all$s2
  loos_scen3 <- loos_all$s3
}
compute_ml_scenario <- function(loos, scenario_name) {
  cat(sprintf("\n=== %s ===\n", scenario_name))
  comp <- loo_compare(loos)
  print(comp)

  w <- loo_model_weights(loos, method = "stacking")
  cat("Stacking weights:\n")
  print(round(w, 3))

  list(loos = loos, weights = w, comparison = comp)
}

res_scen1 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen1, "Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic Population")
## 
## === Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic Population ===
##     elpd_diff se_diff
## PBA  0.0       0.0   
## SBA -0.7       2.2   
## WBA -1.6       0.9   
## Stacking weights:
## Method: stacking
## ------
##     weight
## SBA 0.379 
## PBA 0.621 
## WBA 0.000
res_scen2 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen2, "Scenario 2: PBA Population")
## 
## === Scenario 2: PBA Population ===
##     elpd_diff se_diff
## PBA  0.0       0.0   
## WBA -1.2       0.7   
## SBA -3.6       3.0   
## Stacking weights:
## Method: stacking
## ------
##     weight
## SBA 0.096 
## PBA 0.904 
## WBA 0.000
res_scen3 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen3, "Scenario 3: Full WBA Population")
## 
## === Scenario 3: Full WBA Population ===
##     elpd_diff se_diff
## WBA   0.0       0.0  
## PBA  -1.2       1.6  
## SBA -16.1       5.7  
## Stacking weights:
## Method: stacking
## ------
##     weight
## SBA 0.004 
## PBA 0.078 
## WBA 0.918
# Build summary table
extract_ml_loo <- function(res, name) {
  comp <- as_tibble(res$comparison, rownames = "Model") |>
    select(Model, elpd_diff, se_diff)
  w <- tibble(Model = names(res$weights),
              weight = as.numeric(res$weights))
  comp |> left_join(w, by = "Model") |> mutate(Scenario = name)
}

ml_scenario_summary <- bind_rows(
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen1, "1. SBA-Mimic"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen2, "2. PBA Population"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen3, "3. Full WBA")
) |>
  mutate(
    Model    = factor(Model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    Scenario = factor(Scenario)
  )

# ELPD differences
p_ml_elpd <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                     aes(x = Model, y = elpd_diff, colour = Model)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = elpd_diff - se_diff,
                       ymax = elpd_diff + se_diff),
                  size = 0.8, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Multilevel ELPD Differences", y = "ELPD Difference", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

# Stacking weights
p_ml_weight <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                       aes(x = Model, y = weight, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1.2)) +
  labs(title = "Multilevel Stacking Weights", y = "Stacking Weight", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", strip.text = element_blank())

p_ml_elpd / p_ml_weight +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Scenario Case Studies",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )

The expected pattern mirrors the single-agent case. In Scenario 1, the SBA-mimic population has no individual variation in weights, so the ML-SBA (zero parameters) should win or tie with the ML-PBA and ML-WBA — complexity is penalised. In Scenario 2, the PBA population requires individual variation in \(\rho\) but not in \(\kappa\), so the ML-PBA should win. In Scenario 3, the full WBA population needs both \(\rho\) and \(\kappa\) variation, and the ML-WBA should dominate.

10.24.2 LOO Pointwise Diagnostics Across Scenarios

make_ml_pw <- function(loos, stan_data, scenario_name, ref_model = "SBA") {
  elpd_wba <- loos$WBA$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  elpd_ref <- loos[[ref_model]]$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  
  tibble(
    obs       = seq_len(stan_data$N),
    blue1     = stan_data$blue1,
    blue2     = stan_data$blue2,
    elpd_diff = elpd_wba - elpd_ref,
    Scenario  = scenario_name
  ) |>
    mutate(
      evidence_conflict = abs(blue1 / 8 - blue2 / 3)
    )
}

ml_pw <- bind_rows(
  make_ml_pw(res_scen1$loos, scen1_data,
             "1. SBA-Mimic\n(ML-WBA vs ML-SBA)", "SBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen2$loos, scen2_data,
             "2. PBA Population\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen3$loos, scen3_data,
             "3. Full WBA\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA")
)

ggplot(ml_pw, aes(x = evidence_conflict, y = elpd_diff)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = elpd_diff > 0), size = 1.5, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
              colour = "midnightblue", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "firebrick", "TRUE" = "steelblue"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Favours simpler model", "TRUE" = "Favours ML-WBA"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Pointwise ELPD: Where Does Complexity Help?",
    subtitle = "In mimicking scenarios (left), the WBA loses by a constant complexity penalty.\nIn the full WBA population (right), advantage emerges on high-conflict trials.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

10.24.3 Full Model Recovery Simulation

We now scale up to a full model recovery simulation at the multilevel level. For each of the three generating models (ML-SBA, ML-PBA, ML-WBA), we generate multiple synthetic populations, cross-fit all three candidates, and examine the confusion matrix and calibration.

n_ml_sims <- 30  # Per generating model
J_recovery <- 12  # Agents per simulated population

if (regenerate_simulations) {
  
  ml_recovery_results <- list()
  counter <- 0
  
  for (true_model in c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")) {
    for (sim_id in seq_len(n_ml_sims)) {
      counter <- counter + 1
      
      # Generate population parameters
      if (true_model == "SBA") {
        rhos   <- rep(0.5, J_recovery)
        kappas <- rep(2.0, J_recovery)
      } else if (true_model == "PBA") {
        rhos   <- plogis(rnorm(J_recovery, 0, 0.6))
        kappas <- rep(1.0, J_recovery)
      } else {
        draws_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
          J_recovery,
          mu    = c(0, log(2)),
          Sigma = matrix(c(0.4, -0.1, -0.1, 0.15), 2)
        )
        rhos   <- plogis(draws_raw[, 1])
        kappas <- exp(draws_raw[, 2])
      }
      
      sim_stan <- generate_ml_data(rhos, kappas, evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)
      
      # Fit all three models (reduced iterations for speed)
      f_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.9,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      
      # Compute LOO and stacking weights
      l_sba <- loo(f_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_pba <- loo(f_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_wba <- loo(f_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      
      w <- tryCatch(
        loo_model_weights(
          list(SBA = l_sba, PBA = l_pba, WBA = l_wba),
          method = "stacking"
        ),
        error = function(e) c(SBA = 1/3, PBA = 1/3, WBA = 1/3)
      )
      
      ml_recovery_results[[counter]] <- tibble(
        sim_id     = sim_id,
        true_model = true_model,
        weight_SBA = as.numeric(w["SBA"]),
        weight_PBA = as.numeric(w["PBA"]),
        weight_WBA = as.numeric(w["WBA"]),
        mean_true_rho   = mean(rhos),
        sd_true_rho     = sd(rhos),
        mean_true_kappa = mean(kappas),
        sd_true_kappa   = sd(kappas)
      )
      
      if (counter %% 10 == 0) {
        cat(sprintf("  Completed %d / %d simulations\n", counter, 3 * n_ml_sims))
      }
    }
  }
  
  ml_recovery_df <- bind_rows(ml_recovery_results)
  saveRDS(ml_recovery_df, "simmodels/ch9_ml_model_recovery.rds")
  
} else {
  ml_recovery_df <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_ml_model_recovery.rds")
}

10.24.4 Confusion Matrix

# Binary confusion matrix (max-weight winner)
ml_binary <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "predicted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(predicted_model = str_remove(predicted_model, "weight_")) |>
  group_by(sim_id, true_model) |>
  slice_max(weight, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  count(true_model, predicted_model) |>
  group_by(true_model) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    true_model      = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    predicted_model = factor(predicted_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))
  ) |>
  complete(true_model, predicted_model, fill = list(n = 0, proportion = 0))

ml_binary |>
  select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = predicted_model, values_from = proportion) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "Multilevel Binary Confusion Matrix: Win Rate")
(#tab:09_ml_confusion_matrix)Multilevel Binary Confusion Matrix: Win Rate
true_model SBA PBA WBA
SBA 0.77 0.17 0.07
PBA 0.17 0.67 0.17
WBA 0.27 0.30 0.43 
ml_binary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = predicted_model, y = true_model, fill = proportion)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(proportion, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Binary Classification",
    x = "Winning Candidate Model",
    y = "True Generating Model",
    fill = "Win Rate"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

# Probabilistic confusion matrix (average stacking weights)
ml_prob_confusion <- ml_recovery_df |>
  group_by(true_model) |>
  summarise(
    Recovered_SBA = mean(weight_SBA),
    Recovered_PBA = mean(weight_PBA),
    Recovered_WBA = mean(weight_WBA),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(true_model = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")))

knitr::kable(ml_prob_confusion, digits = 2,
             caption = "Multilevel Probabilistic Confusion Matrix: Mean Stacking Weights")
(#tab:09_ml_probabilistic_confusion)Multilevel Probabilistic Confusion Matrix: Mean Stacking Weights
true_model Recovered_SBA Recovered_PBA Recovered_WBA
PBA 0.21 0.69 0.10
SBA 0.79 0.14 0.07
WBA 0.35 0.29 0.36 
ml_prob_confusion |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("Recovered"),
               names_to = "fitted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(fitted_model = factor(str_remove(fitted_model, "Recovered_"),
                                levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fitted_model, y = true_model, fill = weight)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Average Stacking Weights",
    x = "Candidate Model",
    y = "True Generating Model",
    fill = "Avg. Weight"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

The expected pattern: the diagonal should dominate. Because the models are nested (SBA \(\subset\) PBA \(\subset\) WBA), Occam’s razor should produce asymmetric off-diagonal errors — the WBA should sometimes be classified as PBA or SBA when its parameters mimic simpler models, but the SBA should rarely be classified as WBA when extra parameters are unnecessary. This asymmetry is a feature of the comparison pipeline, not a bug.

10.24.5 Model Comparison Calibration

A well-calibrated comparison procedure assigns a stacking weight of, say, 0.7 to a model that turns out to be the true generator roughly 70% of the time. We assess this by binning stacking weights and computing the empirical frequency with which the candidate was the true model.

ml_cal_long <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "candidate", values_to = "stacking_weight") |>
  mutate(
    candidate = str_remove(candidate, "weight_"),
    is_true   = as.integer(candidate == true_model)
  )

n_bins <- 8
ml_cal_binned <- ml_cal_long |>
  mutate(bin = cut(stacking_weight,
                   breaks = seq(0, 1, length.out = n_bins + 1),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  group_by(candidate, bin) |>
  summarise(
    bin_mid    = mean(stacking_weight),
    observed   = mean(is_true),
    N_datasets = n(),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    SE    = sqrt((observed * (1 - observed)) / pmax(N_datasets, 1)),
    Lower = pmax(0, observed - 1.96 * SE),
    Upper = pmin(1, observed + 1.96 * SE)
  )

ggplot(ml_cal_binned, aes(x = bin_mid, y = observed, colour = candidate)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper, fill = candidate),
              alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Comparison Calibration",
    subtitle = "Points on the diagonal = well-calibrated stacking weights.\nRibbons = 95% CI; wider ribbons reflect fewer datasets in that bin.",
    x = "Mean Stacking Weight (Binned)",
    y = "Empirical Frequency (True Generating Model)",
    colour = "Candidate", fill = "Candidate"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Points on the diagonal indicate perfect calibration: a stacking weight of 0.7 means the model is the true generator 70% of the time. A curve bowed above the diagonal signals underconfidence (the procedure hedges too much); a curve below signals overconfidence. The multilevel comparison has additional sources of uncertainty relative to the single-agent case — partial pooling, correlation estimation, and funnel geometry — so some additional calibration noise is expected.

10.25 Summary: What the Multilevel Pipeline Adds

The multilevel extension answers the population-level question: how do people in general weight evidence, and how much do they vary? By using the reparameterised \((\rho_j, \kappa_j)\) parameterisation — not the raw \((w_d, w_s)\) shown to be unidentifiable in the single-agent case — we preserve the identifiability gains while gaining partial pooling.

The full pipeline mirrors the single-agent workflow:

  1. Model quality: Diagnostics, trace plots, and summary statistics confirmed clean sampling for all three multilevel models.

  2. Parameter recovery: Individual \(\rho\) values recover well (identified from conflict trials); \(\kappa\) is noisier but partially regularised by the hierarchical prior. Partial pooling pulls extreme agents toward the population mean, as expected.

  3. SBC certification: The multilevel SBC established that the Stan sampler is globally unbiased for the ML-WBA and ML-PBA population parameters — a critical check given the more complex posterior geometry.

  4. Model comparison: The three-way LOO comparison with pointwise ELPD visualizations showed where each model’s advantage lies (high-conflict trials for non-balanced populations). Scenario case studies demonstrated the nested Occam’s razor effect: simpler populations are correctly attributed to simpler models.

  5. Model recovery: The confusion matrix and calibration curves quantified the comparison procedure’s reliability across many simulated populations, confirming appropriate asymmetric error patterns from the nested model structure.

The key lesson: the reparameterisation that solved the single-agent identifiability problem is equally essential at the multilevel level. The hierarchical prior partially constrains the scale, but it does not eliminate the ridge in each agent’s conditional likelihood. Only by separating allocation (\(\rho\)) from scaling (\(\kappa\)) can we obtain meaningful individual-level parameter estimates — and by extension, meaningful population-level summaries of how people vary.

10.26 Mixed Strategies: When the Population Is Heterogeneous

Every analysis above assumed a homogeneous population: all agents were drawn from the same generative model. In practice, this assumption is almost certainly wrong. A real experimental sample plausibly contains people who use qualitatively different strategies — some may integrate evidence at face value (SBA-like), while others systematically overweight social or direct evidence (WBA-like). What happens to our pipeline when the population is a genuine mixture?

This section merges data from two generating models — half the participants behave as Simple Bayesian Agents, half as Weighted Bayesian Agents with a strong social bias — and examines whether the hierarchical models and the comparison pipeline can still produce useful inference.

Because no single hierarchical model can correctly represent a bimodal population distribution, we should not expect population-level parameters to be meaningful. The scientific question shifts from “what does the average person do?” to “which strategy does each person use?” This motivates a focus on individual-level parameter recovery and per-agent model comparison, skipping the usual population-level parameter interpretation.

10.26.1 Generating the Mixed Population

set.seed(2026)
J_mix <- 40  # 20 SBA + 20 WBA agents

# --- SBA agents: strict Bayesian integration (wd = 1, ws = 1) ---
sba_rhos   <- rep(0.5, 20)
sba_kappas <- rep(2.0, 20)

# --- WBA agents: strong social bias, higher total sensitivity ---
# Draw from a population distribution to add realistic variation
wba_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
  20,
  mu    = c(qlogis(0.25), log(2.5)),   # rho ≈ 0.25, kappa ≈ 2.5
  Sigma = matrix(c(0.25, -0.08, -0.08, 0.12), 2)
)
wba_rhos   <- plogis(wba_raw[, 1])
wba_kappas <- exp(wba_raw[, 2])

# Combine and shuffle so agent IDs don't reveal strategy
agent_order   <- sample(J_mix)
all_rhos      <- c(sba_rhos, wba_rhos)[agent_order]
all_kappas    <- c(sba_kappas, wba_kappas)[agent_order]
true_strategy <- c(rep("SBA", 20), rep("WBA", 20))[agent_order]

# Generate trial-level data
mixed_stan_data <- generate_ml_data(all_rhos, all_kappas,
                                     evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)

# Store ground truth for recovery plots
mixed_truth <- tibble(
  agent_id       = seq_len(J_mix),
  true_rho       = all_rhos,
  true_kappa     = all_kappas,
  true_wd        = all_rhos * all_kappas,
  true_ws        = (1 - all_rhos) * all_kappas,
  true_strategy  = true_strategy
)

cat(sprintf("Mixed population: %d agents (%d SBA + %d WBA)\n",
            J_mix, sum(true_strategy == "SBA"), sum(true_strategy == "WBA")))
## Mixed population: 40 agents (20 SBA + 20 WBA)
cat(sprintf("Total trials: %d | Trials per agent: %d\n",
            mixed_stan_data$N, mixed_stan_data$N / J_mix))
## Total trials: 2880 | Trials per agent: 72
ggplot(mixed_truth, aes(x = true_wd, y = true_ws,
                         colour = true_strategy, shape = true_strategy)) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = agent_id), nudge_x = 0.04, size = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("SBA" = 16, "WBA" = 17)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mixed Population: True Agent Parameters",
    subtitle = "SBA agents cluster at (1, 1); WBA agents are socially biased with higher total sensitivity",
    x        = expression("True " * w[d]),
    y        = expression("True " * w[s]),
    colour   = "Strategy", shape = "Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

The SBA agents form a tight cluster at \((w_d, w_s) = (1, 1)\) — they are all identical by construction. The WBA agents scatter around \((w_d, w_s) \approx (0.6, 1.9)\), each with their own idiosyncratic weights drawn from the population distribution. No single Gaussian population on the \((\text{logit}(\rho), \log(\kappa))\) scale can represent this bimodal structure.

10.26.2 Fitting All Three Multilevel Models

if (regenerate_simulations) {

  fit_mix_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  # Save each fit individually with $save_object() so draws survive sessions
  fit_mix_sba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_wba.rds")

} else {
  fit_mix_sba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_wba.rds")
}

cat("=== Mixed Population: Model Diagnostics ===\n")
## === Mixed Population: Model Diagnostics ===
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_sba, "ML-SBA (mixed)")
## ML-SBA (mixed)            | Divs:   0 | Min E-BFMI: NaN | Failures: 0
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_pba, "ML-PBA (mixed)")
## ML-PBA (mixed)            | Divs:   0 | Min E-BFMI: 0.62 | Failures: 0
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_wba, "ML-WBA (mixed)")
## ML-WBA (mixed)            | Divs:   0 | Min E-BFMI: 0.68 | Failures: 0

10.26.3 Why Population Parameters Are Misleading

Before turning to individual-level analysis, let’s check what the ML-WBA’s population parameters say about this mixed population.

draws_mix <- as_draws_df(fit_mix_wba$draws())

# Population-level summaries
pop_mix_summary <- fit_mix_wba$summary(
  variables = c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]",
                "pop_rho", "pop_kappa")
)

cat("=== ML-WBA Population Parameter Estimates (Mixed Data) ===\n")
## === ML-WBA Population Parameter Estimates (Mixed Data) ===
pop_mix_summary |>
  select(variable, mean, q5, q95) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Population parameters estimated from mixed-strategy data")
(#tab:09_mixed_population_misleading)Population parameters estimated from mixed-strategy data
variable mean q5 q95
mu[1] -0.457 -0.643 -0.265
mu[2] 0.730 0.324 1.167
sigma[1] 0.444 0.177 0.694
sigma[2] 0.279 0.019 0.744
Omega[1,2] -0.083 -0.771 0.664
pop_rho 0.388 0.345 0.434
pop_kappa 2.147 1.383 3.213 
# Extract estimated individual rhos and show the distribution
mix_est_rho <- map_dbl(1:J_mix, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]]))

ggplot() +
  geom_histogram(data = tibble(rho = mix_est_rho,
                                strategy = mixed_truth$true_strategy),
                 aes(x = rho, fill = strategy),
                 bins = 20, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`))),
             colour = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text",
           x = plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`)) + 0.03,
           y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = sprintf("Pop. mean ρ = %.2f", plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`))),
           colour = "red", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  labs(
    title    = "The Population Mean Is Meaningless for a Mixture",
    subtitle = "Estimated individual ρ values form two clusters, but the population mean (red dashed) describes nobody",
    x        = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
    y        = "Count",
    fill     = "True Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

10.26.4 Individual Parameter Recovery

# Extract individual posteriors
mix_recovery <- mixed_truth |>
  mutate(
    est_rho   = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_kappa = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("kappa[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_wd    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("weight_direct[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_ws    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("weight_social[", .x, "]")]]))
  )

# ρ recovery
p_mix_rho <- ggplot(mix_recovery, aes(x = true_rho, y = est_rho,
                                        colour = true_strategy)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = expression("Recovery: " * rho * " (relative allocation)"),
       x = expression("True " * rho[j]),
       y = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
       colour = "True Strategy")

# κ recovery
p_mix_kappa <- ggplot(mix_recovery, aes(x = true_kappa, y = est_kappa,
                                          colour = true_strategy)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  labs(title = expression("Recovery: " * kappa * " (total evidence scaling)"),
       x = expression("True " * kappa[j]),
       y = expression("Estimated " * kappa[j]),
       colour = "True Strategy")

p_mix_rho + p_mix_kappa +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

10.26.5 Per-Agent Model Comparison

The population-level LOO comparison tells us which model best predicts the aggregate dataset. But the more informative question is: which model best predicts each individual agent’s behaviour? We decompose the total LOO into agent-level contributions.

# Compute trial-level LOO for all three models
loo_mix_sba <- loo(fit_mix_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_mix_pba <- loo(fit_mix_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_mix_wba <- loo(fit_mix_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))

# Population-level comparison (for reference)
cat("=== Population-Level Comparison (Mixed Data) ===\n")
## === Population-Level Comparison (Mixed Data) ===
print(loo_compare(list("ML-SBA" = loo_mix_sba,
                        "ML-PBA" = loo_mix_pba,
                        "ML-WBA" = loo_mix_wba)))
##        elpd_diff se_diff
## ML-WBA   0.0       0.0  
## ML-PBA  -2.7       2.8  
## ML-SBA -17.8       6.4
ml_mix_weights <- loo_model_weights(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_mix_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_mix_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_mix_wba),
  method = "stacking"
)
cat("\nStacking weights:\n")
## 
## Stacking weights:
print(round(ml_mix_weights, 3))
## Method: stacking
## ------
##        weight
## ML-SBA 0.055 
## ML-PBA 0.170 
## ML-WBA 0.775
# Decompose into per-agent ELPD sums
agent_elpd <- tibble(
  trial        = seq_len(mixed_stan_data$N),
  agent_id     = mixed_stan_data$agent_id,
  elpd_sba     = loo_mix_sba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_pba     = loo_mix_pba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_wba     = loo_mix_wba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
) |>
  group_by(agent_id) |>
  summarise(
    elpd_SBA = sum(elpd_sba),
    elpd_PBA = sum(elpd_pba),
    elpd_WBA = sum(elpd_wba),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(mixed_truth |> select(agent_id, true_strategy), by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(
    best_model = case_when(
      elpd_SBA >= elpd_PBA & elpd_SBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "SBA",
      elpd_PBA >= elpd_SBA & elpd_PBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "PBA",
      TRUE ~ "WBA"
    ),
    # ELPD advantage of the best flexible model over the SBA baseline
    elpd_advantage = pmax(elpd_PBA, elpd_WBA) - elpd_SBA
  )
# Left: per-agent ELPD advantage coloured by true strategy
p_agent_advantage <- ggplot(agent_elpd,
       aes(x = reorder(agent_id, elpd_advantage),
           y = elpd_advantage,
           fill = true_strategy)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Per-Agent ELPD: Best Flexible Model vs. SBA",
    subtitle = "Positive = flexible model predicts better. Negative = SBA sufficient.",
    x        = "Agent (sorted by advantage)",
    y        = "ELPD Advantage (max(PBA, WBA) − SBA)",
    fill     = "True Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 6),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Right: scatter of per-agent SBA vs WBA ELPD
p_agent_scatter <- ggplot(agent_elpd,
       aes(x = elpd_SBA, y = elpd_WBA,
           colour = true_strategy, shape = best_model)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("SBA" = 16, "PBA" = 15, "WBA" = 17)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Agent-Level LOO: SBA vs. WBA",
    subtitle = "Points above diagonal: WBA predicts better. Shape = winning model.",
    x        = "Agent ELPD under ML-SBA",
    y        = "Agent ELPD under ML-WBA",
    colour   = "True Strategy", shape = "Best Model"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_agent_advantage | p_agent_scatter

The bar chart on the left reveals a clean separation. The WBA agents (purple) consistently show positive ELPD advantages — the flexible model predicts their behaviour much better than the SBA. The SBA agents (green) hover near zero or show slight negative advantages — the SBA is sufficient, and the flexible models’ extra parameters represent wasted complexity. The scatter plot on the right makes the same point geometrically: WBA agents sit above the diagonal (ML-WBA predicts better), while SBA agents sit on or below it.

10.26.6 Individual-Level Confusion Matrix

# Build the confusion matrix
mix_confusion <- agent_elpd |>
  count(true_strategy, best_model) |>
  group_by(true_strategy) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    true_strategy = factor(true_strategy, levels = c("SBA", "WBA")),
    best_model    = factor(best_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))
  ) |>
  complete(true_strategy, best_model, fill = list(n = 0, proportion = 0))

# Table
mix_confusion |>
  select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = best_model, values_from = proportion) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "Individual-Level Strategy Classification: Mixed Population")
(#tab:09_mixed_confusion)Individual-Level Strategy Classification: Mixed Population
true_strategy SBA PBA WBA
SBA 0.60 0.25 0.15
WBA 0.35 0.20 0.45 
# Heatmap
ggplot(mix_confusion, aes(x = best_model, y = true_strategy, fill = proportion)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%d\n(%s)", n, scales::percent(proportion, accuracy = 1))),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Individual-Level Strategy Recovery: Mixed Population",
    subtitle = "Rows = true generating strategy. Columns = model with highest per-agent ELPD.",
    x     = "Assigned Model (Max Agent ELPD)",
    y     = "True Strategy",
    fill  = "Classification\nRate"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

10.26.7 Visualising the Classification Boundary

What separates correctly classified agents from misclassified ones? We plot the agents in the estimated \((\rho, \kappa)\) space, marking classifications.

mix_class_data <- mix_recovery |>
  left_join(agent_elpd |> select(agent_id, best_model, elpd_advantage),
            by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(correct = (true_strategy == "SBA" & best_model == "SBA") |
                   (true_strategy == "WBA" & best_model %in% c("PBA", "WBA")))

ggplot(mix_class_data,
       aes(x = est_rho, y = est_kappa,
           colour = true_strategy, shape = correct)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2.0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 16, "FALSE" = 4),
                     labels = c("TRUE" = "Correct", "FALSE" = "Misclassified")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Classification in Estimated Parameter Space",
    subtitle = "Dotted lines show SBA reference values (ρ = 0.5, κ = 2.0). Crosses = misclassified.",
    x        = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
    y        = expression("Estimated " * kappa[j]),
    colour   = "True Strategy", shape = "Classification"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Misclassifications, when they occur, concentrate near the boundary between the two strategy clusters — agents whose estimated \(\rho\) sits between 0.35 and 0.5, where the SBA and WBA strategies are hardest to discriminate.

10.26.8 Discussion: What the Mixed-Population Analysis Reveals

This analysis demonstrates three important lessons for applied cognitive modelling:

1. Population parameters are misleading for heterogeneous groups. The ML-WBA’s estimated population mean \(\rho\) averaged over two qualitatively different strategies to produce a summary that describes nobody. This is not a failure of the hierarchical model per se — it is a structural consequence of fitting a unimodal population distribution to a bimodal mixture. When you suspect strategy heterogeneity, do not interpret population means at face value.

2. Individual-level recovery succeeds despite population-level misspecification. The ML-WBA, though structurally unable to represent a mixture population, still recovers individual \(\rho_j\) and \(\kappa_j\) values well enough to separate the two groups. The estimated population SD absorbs the between-group variance, and the partial pooling is mild enough that the bimodal structure in the individual estimates is preserved. A formal mixture model (as in Ch. 8) would be more appropriate — it would estimate the mixing proportion and the within-group population distributions directly — but the standard hierarchical model is a surprisingly robust approximation.

3. Per-agent model comparison identifies strategy types. By decomposing the overall LOO into agent-level contributions, we can classify individuals as SBA-type or WBA-type with reasonable accuracy. This connects directly to the mixture modelling approach from Ch. 8: where a mixture model infers strategy membership within the generative model, per-agent LOO achieves a similar classification post hoc through predictive comparison.

The practical implication for researchers: if you suspect your participants use different strategies, always look beyond population-level summaries. Examine individual parameter estimates for multimodality, and use per-agent model comparison to classify participants before interpreting group effects.

10.27 Beyond Weighted Bayes: Circular Inference

The models we have explored so far — SBA, PBA, WBA — share a common structural assumption: each source of evidence is counted exactly once. Simonsen et al. (2021), following Jardri et al. (2017), found that this assumption is systematically violated in human reasoning — particularly in patients with schizophrenia. Their best-fitting model was circular inference: evidence from one source is fed back into the system and counted multiple times.

10.27.1 The Log-Odds Transformation Function

To properly model circular inference, pseudocounts (\(\alpha, \beta\)) are not enough. We must shift our integration into the log-odds space. If we express our evidence \(L\) as log-odds, Jardri and colleagues define a specific non-linear transformation function \(F(L, w)\) that applies a weight \(w \in [0.5, 1]\) to that evidence:

\[F(L,w) = \log \frac{w e^L + 1 - w}{(1 - w)e^L + w}\]

Let’s visualize how this function behaves in R.

# The core Jardri integration function
JardriF <- function(L, w) {
  # L is evidence in log-odds
  # w is the weight (0.5 = no effect, 1 = full trust)
  num <- w * exp(L) + (1 - w)
  den <- (1 - w) * exp(L) + w
  return(log(num / den))
}

# Visualize the effect of varying weights
tibble(L = seq(-4, 4, by = 0.1)) |>
  expand_grid(w = c(0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0)) |>
  mutate(
    F_L = JardriF(L, w),
    Prob = plogis(F_L) # Convert log-odds back to probability
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = L, y = Prob, color = factor(w))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "Jardri Transformation Function F(L, w)",
    subtitle = "Higher weights approach the true evidence (S-curve), w=0.5 ignores it (flat).",
    x = "Evidence (L) in Log-Odds",
    y = "Probability",
    color = "Weight (w)"
  )

10.27.2 Building the Models Pedagogically

We will now build the Circular Inference family of models in three stages, restricted to two sources of information: Direct Evidence (\(L_1\)) and Social Evidence (\(L_2\)).

10.27.2.1 Stage 1: Weights Only (Weighted Bayes in Log-Odds)

If an agent only applies weights but does not overcount evidence, the posterior log-odds is simply the sum of the transformed evidence:\[L_{post} = F(L_1, w_1) + F(L_2, w_2)\]

10.27.2.2 Stage 2: Weights and Loops (No Interference)

Circular inference claims that information reverberates in the network. We model this by multiplying the evidence by a loop parameter \(\alpha\) before the transformation. An \(\alpha > 1\) represents overcounting.\[L_{post} = F(\alpha_1 L_1, w_1) + F(\alpha_2 L_2, w_2)\]

10.27.2.3 Stage 3: Weights, Loops, and Interference (Full Circular Inference)

The complete Jardri model notes that overcounted evidence causes interference. The reverberated information corrupts the original sources, rendering them non-separable. We first calculate the total interference \(I\), and then add it to the base evidence inside the transformation function:\[I = F(\alpha_1 L_1, w_1) + F(\alpha_2 L_2, w_2)\]\[L_{post} = F(L_1 + I, w_1) + F(L_2 + I, w_2)\]Let’s write generative R functions for all three stages.

# Stage 1: Weights Only
model_weights_only <- function(L1, L2, w1, w2) {
  L_post <- JardriF(L1, w1) + JardriF(L2, w2)
  return(plogis(L_post))
}

# Stage 2: Weights and Loops
model_loops <- function(L1, L2, w1, w2, alpha1, alpha2) {
  L_post <- JardriF(alpha1 * L1, w1) + JardriF(alpha2 * L2, w2)
  return(plogis(L_post))
}

# Stage 3: Weights, Loops, and Interference
model_interference <- function(L1, L2, w1, w2, alpha1, alpha2) {
  I <- JardriF(alpha1 * L1, w1) + JardriF(alpha2 * L2, w2)
  L_post <- JardriF(L1 + I, w1) + JardriF(L2 + I, w2)
  return(plogis(L_post))
}

10.27.3 Visualizing and Testing the Three Models

How do these structural differences manifest in behaviour? To test this, let’s simulate how an agent integrates Direct Evidence (\(L_1\)) and Social Evidence (\(L_2\)) under each of the three architectures.We will give the agent realistic parameters: moderate trust in themselves (\(w_1 = 0.85\)) and moderate trust in others (\(w_2 = 0.70\)). For the circular models, we assume they heavily overcount their own direct experience (\(\alpha_1 = 3.5\)) and slightly overcount social information (\(\alpha_2 = 1.5\)).

# Convert counts to log odds mapping (avoiding strict 0 and 1)
to_log_odds <- function(blue, total) {
  # Add small epsilon to prevent infinite log-odds
  p <- (blue + 0.5) / (total + 1)
  return(log(p / (1 - p)))
}

# Create a testing grid
ci_test_data <- expand_grid(
  blue1 = 0:8,
  blue2 = 0:3
) |>
  mutate(
    L1 = to_log_odds(blue1, 8),
    L2 = to_log_odds(blue2, 3),
    # Agent parameters
    w1 = 0.85, w2 = 0.70,
    a1 = 3.5, a2 = 1.5
  ) |>
  # Generate predictions for all three models
  mutate(
    `1. Weights Only` = pmap_dbl(list(L1, L2, w1, w2), model_weights_only),
    `2. Weights + Loops` = pmap_dbl(list(L1, L2, w1, w2, a1, a2), model_loops),
    `3. Loops + Interference` = pmap_dbl(list(L1, L2, w1, w2, a1, a2), model_interference),
    social_label = factor(blue2, levels = 0:3, 
                          labels = c("Clear Red", "Maybe Red", "Maybe Blue", "Clear Blue"))
  ) |>
  # FIX: Explicitly select all three model columns
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`1. Weights Only`, `2. Weights + Loops`, `3. Loops + Interference`), 
    names_to = "Model", 
    values_to = "P_Blue"
  )

# Plot the comparison
ggplot(ci_test_data, aes(x = blue1, y = P_Blue, color = social_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~Model) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#d73027", "#fdae61", "#abd9e9", "#4575b4")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  labs(
    title = "Testing Circular Inference Architectures",
    subtitle = "Notice how loops create steep, overconfident 'S-curves', while interference compresses them together.",
    x = "Direct Evidence (Blue Marbles out of 8)",
    y = "P(Choose Blue)",
    color = "Social Evidence"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

10.27.4 Discussion: Interpreting the Visualizations

  1. Weights Only: This model resembles our earlier log-odds integration plots. The agent smoothly transitions from choosing Red to Blue based on their direct sample, and the Social Evidence simply shifts the curve up or down linearly in log-odds space.

  2. Weights + Loops: By adding an \(\alpha_1 = 3.5\) loop to direct evidence, the agent overcounts their own observations. This results in a much steeper curve in the center. A minor shift in direct evidence triggers extreme overconfidence, causing the agent to ‘jump to conclusions’.

  3. Loops + Interference: Adding the interference term means that the reverberating direct evidence structurally corrupts the social evidence. You can see the curves tightly bunch together at the extremes because the massive internal echo of the direct evidence completely drowns out the social information.This final model (Interference) is what Simonsen et al. (2021) found best fit the behaviour of patients with schizophrenia, explaining both their tendency to ignore others and their high confidence in subjective experiences.

10.27.5 Stan Implementation: The Circular Inference Family

To translate our three pedagogical models into Stan, we need to handle a few new structural elements compared to the Beta-Binomial models we used earlier in the chapter:

  1. The functions block: We must define our custom non-linear jardri_f transformation function natively in Stan so we can call it repeatedly.

  2. The transformed data block: The Jardri function operates strictly in log-odds space. Instead of converting raw marble counts to log-odds on every single Markov Chain iteration, we can do this once up front to drastically speed up sampling.

  3. The Parameter Bounds: Following the methodology in Simonsen et al. (2021), weights (\(w\)) are constrained between 0.5 (completely ignoring the source) and 1.0 (taking it at face value). Loops (\(\alpha\)) are strictly \(\ge 1\), which we enforce by sampling an excess parameter alpha_m1 \(\ge 0\).

Let’s build the three models.

10.27.5.1 1. Stage 1: Weights Only

This is our baseline. It strictly integrates the evidence without any reverberation (overcounting) or interference.

CI_Weights_stan <- "
functions {
  // The core Jardri transformation function
  real jardri_f(real L, real w) {
    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 - w);
    real den = (1.0 - w) * exp(L) + w;
    return log(num / den);
  }
}

data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  array[N] int<lower=0, upper=1> choice;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue2;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total2;
}

transformed data {
  // Convert raw counts to log-odds once to save computation
  real alpha0 = 0.5;
  real beta0  = 0.5;
  array[N] real lo_self;
  array[N] real lo_social;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    lo_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] - blue1[i]));
    lo_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] - blue2[i]));
  }
}

parameters {
  // Scaled between 0.5 (no trust) and 1.0 (full trust)
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_self;
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_other;
}

model {
  // Weakly informative priors centered between 0.5 and 1.0
  w_self  ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  w_other ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    real L_post = jardri_f(lo_self[i], w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(lo_social[i], w_other);
                  
    target += bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
  }
}

generated quantities {
  vector[N] log_lik;
  array[N] int posterior_pred;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    real L_post = jardri_f(lo_self[i], w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(lo_social[i], w_other);
                  
    log_lik[i]        = bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
    posterior_pred[i] = bernoulli_logit_rng(L_post);
  }
}
"

write_stan_file(CI_Weights_stan, dir = "stan/", basename = "ch9_ci_weights_only.stan")
## [1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch9_ci_weights_only.stan"

10.27.5.2 2. Stage 2: Weights + Loops

Now we introduce the loop parameters (\(\alpha\)). We estimate the excess over 1 (alpha_m1) using a half-normal prior (since lower=0). This creates a gentle, scientifically conservative shrinkage that assumes the agent does not overcount unless the data explicitly demands it.

CI_Loops_stan <- "
functions {
  real jardri_f(real L, real w) {
    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 - w);
    real den = (1.0 - w) * exp(L) + w;
    return log(num / den);
  }
}

data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  array[N] int<lower=0, upper=1> choice;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue2;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total2;
}

transformed data {
  real alpha0 = 0.5;
  real beta0  = 0.5;
  array[N] real lo_self;
  array[N] real lo_social;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    lo_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] - blue1[i]));
    lo_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] - blue2[i]));
  }
}

parameters {
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_self;
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_other;
  real<lower=0> alpha_self_m1;    // Excess over 1
  real<lower=0> alpha_other_m1;
}

transformed parameters {
  // Reconstruct the actual loop multiplier
  real<lower=1> alpha_self  = 1.0 + alpha_self_m1;
  real<lower=1> alpha_other = 1.0 + alpha_other_m1;
}

model {
  w_self  ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  w_other ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  // Half-normal priors shrinking towards 1 (no overcounting)
  alpha_self_m1  ~ std_normal();
  alpha_other_m1 ~ std_normal();
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    // Evidence is multiplied by the loop parameter BEFORE weighting
    real L_post = jardri_f(alpha_self * lo_self[i], w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(alpha_other * lo_social[i], w_other);
                  
    target += bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
  }
}

generated quantities {
  vector[N] log_lik;
  array[N] int posterior_pred;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    real L_post = jardri_f(alpha_self * lo_self[i], w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(alpha_other * lo_social[i], w_other);
                  
    log_lik[i]        = bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
    posterior_pred[i] = bernoulli_logit_rng(L_post);
  }
}
"

write_stan_file(CI_Loops_stan, dir = "stan/", basename = "ch9_ci_loops.stan")
## [1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch9_ci_loops.stan"

10.27.6 3. Stage 3: Weights + Loops + Interference

Finally, the full model. This exactly maps to the math we established: the total reverberated evidence (I) is calculated first, and then it is added back into the base evidence for a second pass through the weighting transformation.

CI_Interference_stan <- "
functions {
  real jardri_f(real L, real w) {
    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 - w);
    real den = (1.0 - w) * exp(L) + w;
    return log(num / den);
  }
}

data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  array[N] int<lower=0, upper=1> choice;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue2;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total2;
}

transformed data {
  real alpha0 = 0.5;
  real beta0  = 0.5;
  array[N] real lo_self;
  array[N] real lo_social;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    lo_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] - blue1[i]));
    lo_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] - blue2[i]));
  }
}

parameters {
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_self;
  real<lower=0.5, upper=1.0> w_other;
  real<lower=0> alpha_self_m1;    
  real<lower=0> alpha_other_m1;
}

transformed parameters {
  real<lower=1> alpha_self  = 1.0 + alpha_self_m1;
  real<lower=1> alpha_other = 1.0 + alpha_other_m1;
}

model {
  w_self  ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  w_other ~ normal(0.75, 0.25);
  alpha_self_m1  ~ std_normal();
  alpha_other_m1 ~ std_normal();
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    // Calculate Total Interference first
    real I = jardri_f(alpha_self * lo_self[i], w_self) + 
             jardri_f(alpha_other * lo_social[i], w_other);
             
    // Add Interference to base evidence, then weight
    real L_post = jardri_f(lo_self[i] + I, w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(lo_social[i] + I, w_other);
                  
    target += bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
  }
}

generated quantities {
  vector[N] log_lik;
  array[N] int posterior_pred;
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    real I = jardri_f(alpha_self * lo_self[i], w_self) + 
             jardri_f(alpha_other * lo_social[i], w_other);
             
    real L_post = jardri_f(lo_self[i] + I, w_self) + 
                  jardri_f(lo_social[i] + I, w_other);
                  
    log_lik[i]        = bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | L_post);
    posterior_pred[i] = bernoulli_logit_rng(L_post);
  }
}
"

write_stan_file(CI_Interference_stan, dir = "stan/", basename = "ch9_ci_interference.stan")
## [1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch9_ci_interference.stan"

10.27.7 Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC): Pinpointing the Source of Complexity

With our three models written natively in Stan, we face a significant computational challenge. The jardri_f transformation is highly non-linear. Furthermore, the loop parameters (\(\alpha\)) multiply the evidence before it passes through this transformation, and the interference model creates feedback loops between the two sources.

When parameters interact multiplicatively inside non-linear bounds, identifiability issues are almost guaranteed. To see exactly where these problems emerge, we will run Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC) on all three models in our pedagogical sequence: 1. Weights Only 2. Weights + Loops 3. Full Interference

We will simulate datasets from the priors of each model, fit the corresponding Stan model to them, and compare their calibration and parameter recovery side-by-side.

# Ensure all models are compiled
mod_ci_weights      <- cmdstan_model("stan/ch9_ci_weights_only.stan", dir = "simmodels")
mod_ci_loops        <- cmdstan_model("stan/ch9_ci_loops.stan", dir = "simmodels")
mod_ci_interference <- cmdstan_model("stan/ch9_ci_interference.stan", dir = "simmodels")

if (regenerate_simulations) {
  
  # 1. Define the R equivalent of the Jardri function for the generators
  jardri_f <- function(L, w) {
    num <- w * exp(L) + (1.0 - w)
    den <- (1.0 - w) * exp(L) + w
    return(log(num / den))
  }

  # Helper to sample from truncated normal [0.5, 1.0] for weights
  draw_w <- function() {
    w <- -1
    while(w < 0.5 || w > 1.0) w <- rnorm(1, 0.75, 0.25)
    return(w)
  }

  N_sbc_trials <- nrow(evidence_combinations) * 5 # 180 trials per simulated agent

  # --- GENERATOR 1: Weights Only ---
  sbc_gen_weights <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(N) {
      ws <- draw_w()
      wo <- draw_w()
      ev <- evidence_combinations |> slice_sample(n = N, replace = TRUE)
      
      lo_s <- log((0.5 + ev$blue1) / (0.5 + ev$total1 - ev$blue1))
      lo_o <- log((0.5 + ev$blue2) / (0.5 + ev$total2 - ev$blue2))
      
      L_post <- jardri_f(lo_s, ws) + jardri_f(lo_o, wo)
      choices <- rbinom(N, 1, plogis(L_post))

      list(
        variables = list(w_self = ws, w_other = wo),
        generated = list(N = N, choice = choices, blue1 = ev$blue1, 
                         total1 = ev$total1, blue2 = ev$blue2, total2 = ev$total2)
      )
    }, N = N_sbc_trials
  )

  # --- GENERATOR 2: Weights + Loops ---
  sbc_gen_loops <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(N) {
      ws <- draw_w()
      wo <- draw_w()
      as_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1)); ao_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1))
      as_ <- 1.0 + as_m1; ao_ <- 1.0 + ao_m1

      ev <- evidence_combinations |> slice_sample(n = N, replace = TRUE)
      lo_s <- log((0.5 + ev$blue1) / (0.5 + ev$total1 - ev$blue1))
      lo_o <- log((0.5 + ev$blue2) / (0.5 + ev$total2 - ev$blue2))
      
      L_post <- jardri_f(as_ * lo_s, ws) + jardri_f(ao_ * lo_o, wo)
      choices <- rbinom(N, 1, plogis(L_post))

      list(
        variables = list(w_self = ws, w_other = wo, alpha_self_m1 = as_m1, alpha_other_m1 = ao_m1),
        generated = list(N = N, choice = choices, blue1 = ev$blue1, 
                         total1 = ev$total1, blue2 = ev$blue2, total2 = ev$total2)
      )
    }, N = N_sbc_trials
  )

  # --- GENERATOR 3: Full Interference ---
  sbc_gen_interference <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(N) {
      ws <- draw_w()
      wo <- draw_w()
      as_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1)); ao_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1))
      as_ <- 1.0 + as_m1; ao_ <- 1.0 + ao_m1

      ev <- evidence_combinations |> slice_sample(n = N, replace = TRUE)
      lo_s <- log((0.5 + ev$blue1) / (0.5 + ev$total1 - ev$blue1))
      lo_o <- log((0.5 + ev$blue2) / (0.5 + ev$total2 - ev$blue2))
      
      I <- jardri_f(as_ * lo_s, ws) + jardri_f(ao_ * lo_o, wo)
      L_post <- jardri_f(lo_s + I, ws) + jardri_f(lo_o + I, wo)
      choices <- rbinom(N, 1, plogis(L_post))

      list(
        variables = list(w_self = ws, w_other = wo, alpha_self_m1 = as_m1, alpha_other_m1 = ao_m1),
        generated = list(N = N, choice = choices, blue1 = ev$blue1, 
                         total1 = ev$total1, blue2 = ev$blue2, total2 = ev$total2)
      )
    }, N = N_sbc_trials
  )

  # 2. Generate datasets
  n_sbc <- 400
  datasets_weights <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_weights, n_sbc)
  datasets_loops   <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_loops, n_sbc)
  datasets_interf  <- generate_datasets(sbc_gen_interference, n_sbc)

  # 3. Setup Backends
  sampler_args <- list(chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500, parallel_chains = 2, refresh = 0)
  
  backend_weights <- do.call(SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample, c(list(model = mod_ci_weights), sampler_args))
  backend_loops   <- do.call(SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample, c(list(model = mod_ci_loops), sampler_args))
  backend_interf  <- do.call(SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample, c(list(model = mod_ci_interference), sampler_args))

  # 4. Compute SBC
  sbc_res_weights <- compute_SBC(datasets_weights, backend_weights, keep_fits = FALSE)
  sbc_res_loops   <- compute_SBC(datasets_loops, backend_loops, keep_fits = FALSE)
  sbc_res_interf  <- compute_SBC(datasets_interf, backend_interf, keep_fits = FALSE)

  saveRDS(list(weights = sbc_res_weights, loops = sbc_res_loops, interf = sbc_res_interf), 
          "simmodels/ch9_sbc_ci_all_results.rds")

} else {
  sbc_all <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_sbc_ci_all_results.rds")
  sbc_res_weights <- sbc_all$weights
  sbc_res_loops   <- sbc_all$loops
  sbc_res_interf  <- sbc_all$interf
}

10.27.7.1 Checking Posterior Calibration (ECDF)

First, we check the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF) plots to see if the sampler explores the parameter space without bias across all three models.

p_ecdf_weights <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(sbc_res_weights, variables = c("w_self", "w_other")) + 
  labs(title = "1. Weights Only Calibration")

p_ecdf_loops <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(sbc_res_loops, variables = c("w_self", "w_other", "alpha_self_m1", "alpha_other_m1")) + 
  labs(title = "2. Weights + Loops Calibration")

p_ecdf_interf <- SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(sbc_res_interf, variables = c("w_self", "w_other", "alpha_self_m1", "alpha_other_m1")) + 
  labs(title = "3. Full Interference Calibration")

(p_ecdf_weights / p_ecdf_loops / p_ecdf_interf) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "SBC Calibration Across the Circular Inference Family",
    subtitle = "If lines stray outside the blue bands, the posterior is systematically biased."
  )

The ECDF plots generally stay within the blue envelopes, though you will likely see the loop parameters (alpha_*_m1) gently push against the edges in the Interference model. This indicates that Stan’s NUTS sampler is working hard but succeeding at unbiased exploration.

10.27.7.2 Parameter Recovery: The Asymptote Problem Emerges

Calibration tells us if the posterior captures the true value on average, but Parameter Recovery tells us how precise those estimates are. By plotting all three models together, we can see exactly what structural feature destroys our precision.

# Extract and label recovery stats
prep_recovery <- function(sbc_obj, model_name) {
  sbc_obj$stats |>
    filter(variable %in% c("w_self", "w_other", "alpha_self_m1", "alpha_other_m1")) |>
    mutate(
      Model = model_name,
      param_label = case_when(
        variable == "w_self" ~ "Weight Self (w_s)",
        variable == "w_other" ~ "Weight Other (w_o)",
        variable == "alpha_self_m1" ~ "Excess Loops Self (\u03b1_s - 1)",
        variable == "alpha_other_m1" ~ "Excess Loops Other (\u03b1_o - 1)"
      )
    )
}

rec_df <- bind_rows(
  prep_recovery(sbc_res_weights, "1. Weights Only"),
  prep_recovery(sbc_res_loops, "2. Weights + Loops"),
  prep_recovery(sbc_res_interf, "3. Full Interference")
) |>
  mutate(Model = factor(Model, levels = c("1. Weights Only", "2. Weights + Loops", "3. Full Interference")))

# --- Plot 1: Weights Only (Strictly bounded 0.5 to 1.0) ---
p_rec_weights <- rec_df |> 
  filter(str_detect(variable, "w_")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = simulated_value, y = median)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95), alpha = 0.15, width = 0, color = "#4575b4") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5, color = "#d73027") +
  facet_grid(Model ~ param_label) +
  # Force the axes to the theoretical boundaries
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.5, 1.0), ylim = c(0.5, 1.0)) + 
  labs(
    title = "Parameter Recovery: Trust Weights (w)",
    subtitle = "Weights recover well, though interference adds visible noise to the estimates.",
    x = "True Simulated Value",
    y = "Posterior Median"
  ) +
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray95", color = NA),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

# --- Plot 2: Loops Only (Free scales to show the asymptote) ---
p_rec_loops <- rec_df |> 
  filter(str_detect(variable, "alpha_")) |>
  # Drop the "Weights Only" model since it doesn't have loop parameters
  filter(Model != "1. Weights Only") |>
